tools.name: find_package
tools.description: Find which Haskell package provides a capability you have not installed yet. Query a task in words ("linear regression", "read csv"); returns ranked packages with the -- cabal: build-depends: line. FIRST step for a new capability; once installed, use find_function.

tools.name: find_function
tools.description: Find a function by NAME or KEYWORD in the installed packages, or list a module's exports by passing a module name ("DataFrame", "Granite.Svg"). Covers the dataframe and granite plotting APIs. Returns module + signature. To find a function by its TYPE, use find_by_type; for a value you already defined, use list_bindings.

tools.name: find_by_type
tools.description: Find an installed function whose TYPE matches a goal type. Pass a type like "[Int] -> Int" (or a hole "_ :: [Int] -> Int"). Use when you know the type you need but not the name. Differs from find_function, which searches by name/keyword.

tools.name: list_bindings
tools.description: List the values, functions, and types already defined in the notebook session, each with its type. Use BEFORE writing a cell that builds on earlier work, to reuse an existing binding instead of recomputing it. find_function searches library APIs, not your bindings.

tools.name: check_type
tools.description: Get the type of an expression, or the kind/definition of a type or class you ALREADY know, without running it. Pass an expression ("map fst"), a value name, or a type/class name. To find a name you do not know, use find_function or find_by_type.

tools.name: find_example_cell
tools.description: Search runnable example cells for a cell-shape idiom ("read csv", "typed column"); returns source to paste and adapt. To find which package/function does a task, use find_package / find_function.

tools.name: peek_data
tools.description: Inspect a CSV/TSV file before loading it: returns the delimiter, whether row one is a header, the first N rows, and a guessed type per column. Use to shape a DataFrame read instead of guessing the schema.

tools.name: list_cells
tools.description: List every cell: id, type, language, whether it has an error, and its first line. Start here for the notebook's structure. For one cell's full source use read_cell; for all sources at once use export_notebook.

tools.name: read_cell
tools.description: Read one cell's full source AND its outputs/errors, by id. Use after list_cells to inspect a specific cell. If you only need the result, use read_cell_output.

tools.name: read_cell_output
tools.description: Read only a cell's outputs and error (not its source), by id. Use to check what a cell produced or why it failed; use read_cell when you also need the source.

tools.name: find_cells_by_content
tools.description: Find cells whose source contains a substring; returns matching cell ids with context. Use to locate where something is defined or used.

tools.name: export_notebook
tools.description: Return every cell's id, position, type, language, and source in one call — a whole-notebook snapshot in a single request instead of many read_cell calls.

tools.name: insert_cell
tools.description: Append a NEW cell at the end of the notebook and run it. Use to add code or prose. Cannot be used while another cell has an unresolved error — fix that cell (replace_cell_source) or remove it (delete_cell) first. To change an existing cell use replace_cell_source.

tools.name: replace_cell_source
tools.description: Replace the entire source of an existing cell and auto-run it. Use to FIX or iterate on a cell (especially one with an error). Full replacement, not a diff. To add a NEW cell use insert_cell; to suggest a change a user must approve, use propose_edit.

tools.name: delete_cell
tools.description: Remove a cell by id. Use to delete a cell you cannot fix in place — e.g. a failing cell blocking insert_cell — or an abandoned/duplicate cell. Prefer replace_cell_source to fix a cell rather than deleting and re-adding.

tools.name: propose_edit
tools.description: Suggest a source change to a cell the USER authored — NOT applied until the user accepts. Use only for user-owned cells where review matters. For cells you created yourself use replace_cell_source.

tools.name: execute_cell
tools.description: Run an existing cell by id and wait for its result. Use to re-run a cell. To change a cell's source use replace_cell_source; to run code without touching the notebook use scratchpad.

tools.name: scratchpad
tools.description: Run a SELF-CONTAINED snippet in an isolated session that CANNOT see the notebook's cells or bindings (same packages, separate state). Use for a throwaway experiment. To run something that uses notebook bindings, put it in a cell instead.

tools.name: kernel_status
tools.description: Report the kernel's state (cold | idle | executing | building) without blocking, even while a cell is running. Use to tell busy from wedged. To wait for a running cell use await_idle; to recover a wedged kernel use kernel_restart.

tools.name: await_idle
tools.description: Block until the running cell finishes (bounded ~45s), then return the kernel status. Use after starting work to wait for it to settle. Cheaper than polling kernel_status in a loop.

tools.name: interrupt
tools.description: Abort the cell the kernel is currently running. No-op if idle. Try this first when a cell is stuck; if the kernel ignores it, use kernel_restart.

tools.name: kernel_restart
tools.description: Hard-reset the kernel: force-kill the process (even a wedged one) and respawn it clean, reusing installed packages without rebuilding and without re-running cells. Use to recover a kernel interrupt could not free.

tools.name: api_reference
tools.description: Fetch the curated signature card for the dataframe and granite plotting APIs (DataFrame, DataFrame.Functions, Granite.Spec, Granite.Svg). Works even before any cell has run — no live session needed. Pass a module substring, or omit for the whole card. For a live search of installed modules use find_function.

module.description: Module substring, or empty for all.

tools.name: describe_function
tools.description: Show the haddock documentation (the prose explanation) for an installed function or type, by name. Use when you know the name and want to understand what it DOES, beyond its type. For the type alone use check_type; to find a name use find_function.

tools.name: locate_import
tools.description: Given a function/type/operator NAME you already know, return which module exports it and the exact import line to paste. Differs from find_module_capabilities (which ranks fuzzy keyword/task matches and is for discovering an unknown function): this is an authoritative reverse lookup for a known name, and unlike ghci_query op=info it works even when the name is not yet imported into the session. Use it the moment you write a name and aren't sure of its module or package.

name.description: The exact identifier you want to import, e.g. "columnAsList", "scatter", "fromList", "(.|.)". Unqualified; no module prefix.

tools.rationale: Closes the known-name-but-unknown-import gap that forces guessed imports; reverse name->module lookup is a distinct intent from keyword discovery and works before the name is in scope.

tools.name: search_by_type
tools.description: Find functions whose SIGNATURE matches a type you want, e.g. "Text -> DataFrame" or "[Double] -> Double" — true Hoogle-by-type search ranked by how well the arrow shape fits. Differs from find_module_capabilities, whose query is primarily a keyword/task or module name and whose type handling is an unranked substring fallback: pass this tool a type signature (with `->`) when you know the input and output types but not the function name. Use it for 'I have an X, I need a Y' lookups.

signature.description: A type signature containing `->`, e.g. "FilePath -> IO DataFrame", "[a] -> Int", "Double -> Picture". Concrete or polymorphic types both work.

tools.rationale: A type-shape search is a separate intent from keyword search and is far more routable by name than overloading the keyword tool's substring type fallback.

tools.name: show_usage
tools.description: Show a short runnable snippet that CALLS a specific named function, so you can see argument order and a real invocation before writing your own. Differs from find_examples (which searches a small set of curated whole-cell idioms by task keyword, not by function name) and from ghci_query op=doc (which returns prose/signature, never a call site): pass one function name and get a concrete usage line. Use it after you have the signature but are unsure how the arguments go together.

name.description: The exact function name to show a call example for, e.g. "groupBy", "readCsv", "lineGraph". Unqualified.

tools.rationale: Knowing a signature but not how to apply it is a distinct, common failure for a weak model; a per-name call-site example is served by neither the curated cell-idiom search nor the prose/doc query.

tools.name: list_errors
tools.description: List every cell that currently has an error, each with its cell ID, the error line, and the error message, in one call. Use this to see the whole notebook's broken state at a glance; unlike list_cells, which only flags hasError as a boolean per cell, this returns the actual messages so you don't have to call read_cell_output on each failing cell. Returns an empty list when nothing is broken.
tools.rationale: One roll-up of all errored cells + messages replaces the N+1 read_cell_output loop a weak model would otherwise run after seeing hasError flags.

tools.name: resolve_name
tools.description: Find which cell defines a given top-level name (a function, type, or value binding) and whether it is in scope anywhere in the notebook. Use this when you have a name and need its origin cell; unlike find_cells_by_content, which does a raw substring match over all sources, this returns the single cell that canonically DEFINES the name (first-wins) plus the cell IDs that USE it, and unlike ghci_query bindings, which lists live session variables with types but no cell provenance, this maps the name back to its notebook cell. Reports not-defined when no cell binds it.

name.description: The exact top-level identifier to resolve, e.g. "df", "trainModel", "MyRecord".

tools.rationale: Definition-aware name->cell resolution (with users) is the 'where does this come from / is it in scope' question neither substring search nor session-binding listing answers.

tools.name: trace_cell_deps
tools.description: Show the reactive dependency edges of one cell: which cells it depends on (the names it consumes and the cells that define them) and which cells depend on it (its transitive downstream blast radius). Use this before editing or deleting a cell to learn what will re-run or break; no existing tool exposes the dependency graph — list_cells gives a flat positional list and await_idle only waits for a cascade that already started, whereas this predicts the cascade. Pass direction to get just upstream, just downstream, or both.

cell_id.description: The cell whose dependency edges to trace.

direction.description: upstream = cells this one needs; downstream = cells that re-run if this one changes; both = both sets. Default both.

tools.rationale: Exposes the reactivity DAG (defs/uses + transitive reverse-deps) so the model can see 'what feeds this' and 'what re-runs if I touch it' before mutating, which no current tool reveals.
